transcript: playing against Parkinson's and with Parkinson's and confronting fear it's almost a kind of meditation I do when I serve the aim of ping-pong parkinson isn't just to keep patients moving but also to ward off loneliness there's a lot of people just sitting in their houses who can't get out. It's both a training group and a self-help group. The Ping-Pong Parkinson meet-up in Fulda in the west of Germany. Every week, ten players with the disease meet here. The idea originally comes from the US. Silke Kind founded this group in 2020 and plays here every week. It's a ritual and a liberation from the daily Parkinson's drudge, where you're constantly reminded that you have difficulty moving. You enter this completely new world, where everything else around you fades away. Silke Kind is 56 and has had Parkinson's disease for 10 years. She started playing ping pong as a teenager and played in the club for years. Parkinson's brought her back to the table. With Parkinson's, the brain can no longer control the body's movements well. The body becomes slower, stiffer, and an involuntary tremor sets in. The disease can't be cured, but exercise can help. And dopamine is crucial. That's a messenger substance for signals between the nerve cells in the brain. My dopamine. You have to take what the body doesn't produce I take my dopamine every two hours and that's very important because otherwise my energy runs out a car can't run without fuel and I can't do anything without dopamine. I can't even think or move properly. That's why it's very important to take it at regular intervals, so you don't have an energy drop. Silke doesn't just play. She's also one of two trainers in her Parkinson's group. I'd say we can start right away and do a few sets, so we learn how to count a set correctly. Silke Kind volunteers for Ping Pong Parkinson. She's in charge of coordinating a huge network of training groups all around Germany. It's a lot of work. More and more people are joining, including in Fua. Wow what a backhand. Volkmar Pretin is here for the second time. Ping-pong Parkinson was a tip from his neurologist. So how do you like it? The important thing for me was to get to know some like-minded people, to find out how they deal with it all. I've had it for six or seven years, I don't know exactly. And how do you feel when you're playing table tennis? Can you get into the game completely? I think that's what it's all about, that you don't think about the tremors anymore. Sometimes I still shake, even playing just now, but after the second or third game, it's gone. Silke is convinced that table tennis can slow down the progression of Parkinson's, but it hasn't been scientifically recognized yet that the game can help with the brain condition. I really think this is a workout for the brain because the player does so many different strokes. You're constantly deciding how to receive the ball, how to play it back and where you want want it to land. There are so many processes going on in the brain, lots of tiny thought processes. And I think that's good training, because it keeps the brain in constant motion. My symptoms are mainly a tremor and stiffness here in the right half of my body. It sometimes feels like there's a dividing line running all the way through my body and the right side starts to shake and get stiff. Since her diagnosis, Silke Kind has been trying to do as much as she can every day, while it's still possible, she says. It's fear that drives you to keep moving. You've no choice. You can get sucked down into this whirlpool, be pulled under. But I feel life is so short. You've just got to enjoy the time you've got left. I don't want to be sitting around with dark thoughts." She's determined to enjoy life, but above all to keep fighting back against Parkinson's. Today Silke has her weekly appointment with occupational therapist Silas Barth. It's strength and fine motor skills training. And now make a fingerprint in the plasticine with each finger to train the strength of individual fingers. This is good for selective finger mobility, the ability of each finger to move separately. It's hard. It's really hard. I'd rather use the other hand now. I bet. But Silke isn't easy on herself and always keeps moving. Right after occupational therapy, it's off to physiotherapy. But first, she refuels with dopamine. everything that gets tense and hunched up in the day, open it up and up and up. Physiotherapy with Nadia Tsela is all about balance and posture. Movement is very important for Parkinson's patients. They need several units of movement a week to keep the body stimulated and to delay the physical degeneration as long as possible. In Zicke's case, ping-pong is a very important part of her everyday life. It promotes hand-eye coordination. Balance becomes more stable. She's more confident when she walks. We don't yet have the scientific studies that are often demanded to be able to say, here's the method for improving XYZ. But sometimes you have to listen to and respect patients' actual lived experience. You can't always measure everything. Even after 10 years with the disease, Silke is constantly having to adapt to her Parkinson's. Small movements are difficult. I do have to concentrate. There have been quite a few accidents, which ended bloodily. I feel like a pilot sitting in a plane, where the plane is constantly drifting down or maybe up again, and I keep having to use my lever to regain balance and keep the plane on course." Silke got Parkinson's at 46, unusually early. For her, the diagnosis came out of the blue. It felt a bit like someone telling me that the weather would be good tomorrow, or bad, because I had no idea what the disease meant. I told my husband right away. He always tries to stay calm. Then early on, there were these sudden moments where I just felt utter panic. I'd say, we have to leave our house now. There are stairs everywhere. I won't be able to climb them anymore at some point. We have to prepare for that. Those were the kind of panic attacks I had. And he'd say, look, nothing's happened yet. Let's wait. We can always do it later. Silke has been married to her husband, Christoph, for almost 30 years. They have two grown-up children. Christoph is a lawyer. Silke works in his office every day. At home Parkinson's syndrome and how to deal with it often comes up. I think at first it's a blow to your self-esteem when you know that if you take too much medication and you're in a social situation you might suddenly have these shakes. It doesn't bother me. But it's terrible for her, because she thinks everyone is looking at her. I always say, who cares if people are looking or not? Let them look. Everyone has limitations as they get older. I'm ten years older than her, and I'm noticing that I can do less here and there. But with this disease, the less is probably a bit more intense and premature. Of course, there might be a wheelchair at some point. But it might be me who needs a wheelchair, you never know. Let's see who ends up pushing who I was counting on my wife pushing me get your bets in now but first it's back to the ping-pong table for silke this this time at the tournament in Weißwasser, all the way in the east of Germany. Silke has traveled almost 500 kilometers for this. She plays in every tournament. It shows me again and again that despite the disease everything is possible and you can achieve top performances. Of course, everything has to be just right and sometimes Parkinson's gets in the way, but you have to learn to live with that, not get frustrated and keep going in spite of it." A lot of ping-pong Parkinson players bring their families along to the tournaments. Here in the sports hall, it's all about community. For Silke and for many others here, Ping Pong Parkinson gave them a new lease on life. A way to win the small battles in the war against an incurable disease if I hadn't had this disease I wouldn't have experienced all this and the sense of community and all these people I couldn't even miss them all such fantastic people I would say that these are real everyday heroes they can deal with their fate and still live a happy life. They are all role models for me.
